text:
RADIOLOGY REPORT

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [REDACTED]
ORDERING PROVIDER: Medical Oncology
EXAMINATION: (1) Technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP) whole body bone scintigraphy with SPECT/CT of the lumbar spine and pelvis; (2) CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast
CLINICAL INDICATION: 69-year-old male with known metastatic castration-sensitive prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5), T3bN1M1b at diagnosis, with known osseous metastases (L3, L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac crest, right sacral ala) and right external iliac lymphadenopathy. Currently on androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide acetate and abiraterone acetate/prednisone. Restaging at 18-month treatment mark. PSA 1.1 ng/mL (rising from nadir of 0.8 ng/mL). Evaluate for treatment response and disease status.
COMPARISON: Tc-99m bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 15 months prior (at diagnosis workup, approximately 3 months prior to treatment initiation).

---

TC-99M MDP WHOLE BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT

TECHNIQUE: Following intravenous administration of 25.4 mCi Tc-99m MDP, anterior and posterior whole body planar images were obtained at approximately 3 hours post-injection. Supplemental SPECT/CT imaging of the lumbar spine and pelvis was performed for anatomic correlation. Images reviewed on dedicated nuclear medicine workstation.

FINDINGS:

SKULL / CALVARIUM: No abnormal focal areas of increased or decreased radiotracer uptake. Symmetric radiotracer distribution throughout the cranial vault.

CERVICAL SPINE: Normal radiotracer distribution. No focal lesions.

THORACIC SPINE: Mild degenerative uptake at multiple thoracic disc levels, most pronounced at T6-T7 and T10-T11, without convincing focal lesion to suggest osseous metastatic involvement. Appearances are consistent with spondylosis.

LUMBAR SPINE:
- L3 vertebral body: Previously demonstrated area of moderately increased tracer uptake now shows markedly reduced intensity with a predominantly photopenic (sclerotic) center and mild rim of peripheral activity. This pattern is characteristic of a treated/healing osteoblastic lesion and represents a favorable response compared to prior imaging when the lesion demonstrated uniformly intense tracer avidity. SPECT/CT correlation demonstrates dense sclerosis of the L3 vertebral body corresponding to this area, consistent with a treated metastatic deposit undergoing osseous consolidation.
- L4 vertebral body: Similarly, previously noted area of increased uptake at L4 has substantially diminished in intensity. Residual faint, patchy uptake remains, superimposed on background degenerative changes at the L4-L5 facet joints. SPECT/CT demonstrates sclerotic remodeling of the superior endplate and anterior body of L4. Findings are consistent with a favorable treatment response.
- Remainder of lumbar spine: No new areas of abnormal tracer uptake identified.

SACRUM AND PELVIS:
- Right sacral ala: Previously seen focus of moderately intense tracer uptake at the right sacral ala is now significantly diminished in activity and appears nearly photopenic centrally, again consistent with sclerotic change in a treated metastatic deposit. SPECT/CT confirms dense sclerotic foci within the right sacral ala without cortical destruction or soft tissue extension.
- Right iliac crest: The previously described lesion at the right iliac crest demonstrates substantially reduced tracer intensity compared to prior study. There is residual faint, mildly heterogeneous uptake in this region, slightly above background, but markedly improved. SPECT/CT again shows sclerotic bony changes at this site without cortical breakthrough.
- Remainder of pelvis: No new foci of abnormal tracer uptake in the bilateral ilia, acetabula, pubic rami, or ischium. Normal uptake in the sacroiliac joints bilaterally, without asymmetry to suggest new involvement.

HIPS AND PROXIMAL FEMORA: Normal symmetric tracer distribution throughout both femoral heads, necks, and intertrochanteric regions. No focal areas of abnormal uptake.

RIBS / STERNUM / CLAVICLES / SCAPULAE: Symmetric, physiologic uptake throughout the thoracic cage. Several mild foci of mildly increased uptake along the posterior rib cortices bilaterally are consistent with prior minor trauma or degenerative change. No focal lesion morphology to suggest metastatic involvement. No superscan pattern. Background soft tissue activity is appropriate.

APPENDICULAR SKELETON (BILATERAL UPPER AND LOWER EXTREMITIES): No focal areas of abnormal radiotracer accumulation in the long bones, hands, or feet bilaterally.

KIDNEYS AND BLADDER: Bilateral kidneys demonstrate symmetric tracer excretion. Urinary bladder is visualized with expected radiotracer concentration. No renal asymmetry.

IMPRESSION - BONE SCINTIGRAPHY:
1. Known osseous metastatic deposits at the L3 vertebral body, L4 vertebral body, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala all show markedly reduced radiotracer avidity with a predominantly sclerotic/photopenic appearance compared to the prior study. These findings are consistent with treatment-related osseous healing response in the setting of ongoing androgen deprivation and abiraterone therapy. No residual features to suggest active osteolytic progression at these sites.
2. No new areas of abnormal increased radiotracer uptake to suggest new osseous metastatic deposits anywhere in the axial or appendicular skeleton.
3. Degenerative changes as described above, without convincing new metastatic lesions.
4. No superscan.

---

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL Omnipaque-350 intravenous contrast via right antecubital vein using a standard portal venous phase protocol. Axial images at 3 mm slice thickness; coronal and sagittal reconstructions reviewed. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and bone windows examined.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

LUNGS AND PLEURA: The lungs are clear without focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or discrete pulmonary nodule. No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. The tracheobronchial tree is patent.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA: The mediastinum is of normal caliber. No enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes by size criteria. No anterior mediastinal mass. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. Thoracic aorta and great vessels appear unremarkable.

CHEST WALL: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. No destructive or lytic bone lesion within the visible thoracic vertebrae or ribs on bone windows.

ABDOMEN:

LIVER: The liver is normal in size with homogeneous enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions. No biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is surgically absent (prior cholecystectomy noted on review, consistent with expected surgical clips in the gallbladder fossa).

SPLEEN: Normal size and enhancement. No focal splenic lesion.

PANCREAS: Pancreas appears unremarkable without pancreatic ductal dilatation or focal mass.

ADRENAL GLANDS: Bilateral adrenal glands are thin and unremarkable. No adrenal nodule or mass. This is notable given the patient's ongoing abiraterone therapy, which is known to cause functional changes but without structural adrenal enlargement in this case.

KIDNEYS: Both kidneys are normal in size with symmetric, prompt cortical enhancement and excretion. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass or renal calculi identified. Note is made of a 4 mm hypodense focus in the upper pole of the right kidney, too small to characterize but without features of complexity.

RETROPERITONEUM: The aorta demonstrates mild atherosclerotic calcification. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Para-aortic and aortocaval lymph nodes are within normal size limits (largest visible para-aortic node approximately 7 mm in short axis).

BOWEL: Small and large bowel loops are normal in caliber and appearance without obstruction, wall thickening, or acute inflammatory change. No free air or free fluid.

PELVIS:

PROSTATE AND SEMINAL VESICLES: The prostate gland remains enlarged at approximately 4.5 cm in greatest transverse dimension (previously 4.8 cm at diagnosis), showing mild interval decrease in size consistent with androgen ablation. No discrete new pelvic mass. The seminal vesicles appear somewhat involuted, also consistent with hormonal treatment effect.

BLADDER: The urinary bladder is adequately distended with homogeneous wall thickness. No intraluminal filling defect or wall thickening to suggest bladder involvement.

LYMPH NODES:
- Right external iliac chain: The previously enlarged right external iliac lymph node measuring 1.8 cm at diagnosis now measures approximately 0.9 cm in short axis. This represents a substantial reduction in size, consistent with a favorable nodal response to systemic therapy.
- Left external iliac chain: No enlarged lymph nodes. Previously unremarkable.
- Right internal iliac chain: No lymphadenopathy.
- Obturator fossae bilaterally: No lymphadenopathy.
- Inguinal regions: No pathologic inguinal lymph nodes.

BONY PELVIS / VISIBLE SPINE: Sclerotic changes at the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies and right iliac crest are concordant with the bone scintigraphy findings described above and are consistent with treated/healing osseous metastases. The right sacral ala demonstrates similar sclerotic changes. No new lytic or destructive bone lesions identified in the visible lumbar spine, sacrum, or pelvis on bone windows.

IMPRESSION - CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS:
1. No visceral metastatic disease. No hepatic lesions, no pulmonary nodules or consolidations, no adrenal masses.
2. Right external iliac lymph node has substantially decreased in size from 1.8 cm to 0.9 cm, consistent with favorable lymph node response to androgen deprivation and abiraterone therapy.
3. Mild interval decrease in prostate gland size consistent with hormone therapy effect.
4. Sclerotic changes at known osseous metastatic sites (L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala) concordant with bone scintigraphy, consistent with osteoblastic healing response.
5. No new lymphadenopathy identified in the retroperitoneum or pelvis.
6. Incidental 4 mm indeterminate renal cortical focus, right kidney upper pole; too small to characterize, likely a simple cyst; no follow-up recommended at this time given oncologic context.

---

OVERALL IMPRESSION:

Radiographically stable disease in a patient with previously documented stage IVB prostate adenocarcinoma undergoing androgen deprivation therapy with abiraterone/prednisone. All previously known osseous metastatic deposits (L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala) demonstrate features of treatment response with markedly reduced tracer avidity on bone scan and sclerotic morphology on CT, consistent with healing osteoblastic response. No new bone metastases. The largest previously enlarged right external iliac lymph node has decreased substantially in size. No visceral metastases identified in the liver, lungs, or adrenal glands. These findings represent a favorable imaging response to systemic therapy. Clinical correlation with PSA trajectory (reported at 1.1 ng/mL, noted to be a slight rise from documented nadir of 0.8 ng/mL) is recommended, as biochemical and radiographic assessments may not always be concordant in the setting of ongoing AR-pathway directed therapy.

Interpreting Radiologist: [SIGNATURE REDACTED]
Nuclear Medicine / Diagnostic Radiology
[Institution Redacted]
Report Electronically Signed
